title: Fatigue, jaundice, and dark urine in a patient with CLL on new medications

chart.oneLiner: 62F with chronic lymphocytic leukemia (watchful-waiting), hypertension on a thiazide, and a recently started antibiotic presents with one week of rapidly worsening fatigue, jaundice, and dark urine.

opening: I've gone really pale and worn out over the past week, my eyes have turned yellow, and my urine looks dark like tea.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 62-year-old woman, pale and mildly jaundiced, tired and a little breathless on exertion. Cooperative, answers what is asked.

HPI: Rapidly progressive fatigue, breathlessness on exertion, and lightheadedness over about a week. Noticed yellowing of the eyes and dark, tea-colored urine over the last few days. About two weeks ago she had a chesty cold and her doctor started her on an antibiotic for a presumed chest infection (a 'Z-pack' type pill, azithromycin) AND a urinary antibiotic a few days later when her urine test looked off (nitrofurantoin) — reveal these only if asked about recent illnesses or new medications. NO black or bloody stools, NO vomiting blood, NO heavy menstrual bleeding (post-menopausal). NO known liver disease, NO heavy alcohol use. She has not been out in the cold or noticed her fingers turning blue/white. NO fever spikes, NO confusion, NO new bruising or nosebleeds.
PMH: chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) diagnosed ~2 years ago, never needed treatment ('watch and wait'), known mildly enlarged lymph nodes; hypertension; mild osteoarthritis of the knees. Reveal CLL only if asked about past medical problems or blood conditions.
MEDS (reveal only if asked about medications): hydrochlorothiazide for blood pressure; recently started azithromycin (chest) and nitrofurantoin (urine) antibiotics; over-the-counter ibuprofen most days for knee pain; no anticoagulants; no warfarin. ALLERGIES: none known.
SOCIAL: retired teacher, minimal alcohol (a glass of wine on holidays), never smoked, no recreational drugs. FAMILY: a brother with 'a blood problem'; no known bleeding disorders. ROS: as above — fatigue, exertional breathlessness, dark urine, yellow eyes; no chest pain, no palpitations she has noticed, no leg swelling, no bleeding.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: pale with scleral icterus/jaundice; mild distress with exertion.
- Vitals: BP 112/70, HR 112, RR 18, 37.5°C, SpO2 97% RA.
- Lymph nodes: a few small, soft, non-tender enlarged cervical and axillary nodes (chronic, from her CLL) if examined.
- Abdomen: moderate splenomegaly if examined; no marked tenderness; liver not enlarged.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, soft systolic flow murmur. No petechiae, NO purpura, no new bruising; no focal neurologic deficit; mucous membranes pale.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the recent chest cold, the new antibiotics, the thiazide, or the ibuprofen unless asked about recent illnesses or medications.
- Do NOT volunteer the CLL or the brother's blood problem unless asked about past medical/blood history or family history.
- Lymphadenopathy and splenomegaly are revealed only if the relevant exam is performed.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.CBC: Hgb 6.9 (severe), MCV 104 (elevated, due to reticulocytosis), WBC 38 with an absolute LYMPHOCYTOSIS (mature small lymphocytes — her known CLL), Plt 245 (NORMAL platelets — important, argues against TTP/HUS and against Evans-type thrombocytopenia).
labs.Reticulocyte: Reticulocyte count MARKEDLY ELEVATED at 9% (absolute ~280 x10^9/L) — an appropriate, brisk marrow response to hemolysis.
labs.Smear: Numerous SPHEROCYTES and polychromasia with smudge cells (consistent with CLL); NO schistocytes (argues strongly against a microangiopathic process / TTP-HUS), no bite cells.
labs.Hemolysis: LDH HIGH (610), indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin HIGH (total bili 4.1 with direct 0.6, so indirect 3.5), haptoglobin UNDETECTABLE — a clear intravascular/extravascular hemolytic pattern.
labs.Coombs: Direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test POSITIVE, strongly positive for IgG with negative C3d (a WARM-antibody pattern) — confirms autoimmune hemolysis, here secondary to her CLL.
labs.BMP: Na 129 (mild HYPONATREMIA — thiazide-related), K 4.2, Cl 96, HCO3 24 (anion gap = 129 - (96 + 24) = 9, NORMAL — no metabolic acidosis), BUN 16, Cr 0.9 (NORMAL renal function — argues against HUS); Mg 1.6 (low-normal), corrected Ca normal.
labs.Coags: PT, aPTT, and fibrinogen NORMAL with a normal D-dimer — argues against DIC as the cause of the hemolysis.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia (~112 bpm) with a markedly PROLONGED QT interval (measured QT ~370 ms at this rate; QTc by Bazett ~505 ms), broad-based T waves; no acute ST-elevation. The prolonged QTc is drug-induced (azithromycin plus a borderline-low magnesium) layered on a demand tachycardia from severe anemia.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: sinus tachycardia with a markedly prolonged QT/QTc (drug-induced), without acute ST-elevation. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse a normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing sinus tachycardia with marked QT prolongation
ekg.clinicalVignette: A patient on a macrolide antibiotic with a low magnesium has a fast rhythm and a long QT interval on the tracing.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Mild mediastinal/hilar widening from lymphadenopathy (consistent with her known CLL); lungs clear with no consolidation, effusion, or pulmonary edema; normal cardiac silhouette.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: mild mediastinal/hilar lymphadenopathy with otherwise clear lungs. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse a normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing mild mediastinal/hilar lymphadenopathy with clear lungs
cxr.clinicalVignette: A patient with a chronic lymphoproliferative disorder has a screening chest film before treatment.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, give your differential for this anemia (note your leading diagnosis). Name at least THREE distinct etiologies or mechanisms of hemolysis you are weighing, and say which can't-miss diagnosis you are most worried about.
steps.rubric: Hemolytic anemia — autoimmune, likely secondary to her CLL (leading,6). Microangiopathic hemolysis — TTP/HUS (CANT-MISS to exclude given hemolysis,5). Drug-induced/oxidant hemolysis given new azithromycin, nitrofurantoin, and chronic NSAID; consider G6PD-type oxidant injury (core,3). DIC as a consumptive hemolysis (core,2). Hereditary/membrane hemolysis (spherocytosis) (core,1). Names ≥3 distinct mechanisms of hemolysis — e.g. antibody-mediated (autoimmune), mechanical/microangiopathic (schistocytes), oxidant/drug-induced, consumptive (DIC), membrane/enzyme defects (core,4). Explicitly states the imperative to look for schistocytes + thrombocytopenia + renal failure to exclude TTP/HUS before treating (core,4). PENALTY: anchoring on simple iron-deficiency or occult-bleeding anemia and missing the hemolytic picture (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA), most likely SECONDARY to her known CLL. Must-not-miss to exclude first: microangiopathic hemolysis — TTP/HUS — because it shares hemolysis but is treated with plasma exchange, not steroids. At least three distinct hemolysis mechanisms to weigh: (1) antibody-mediated/autoimmune (warm IgG, Coombs-positive); (2) mechanical/microangiopathic (schistocytes — TTP/HUS/DIC); (3) oxidant/drug-induced hemolysis from her newly started antibiotics (azithromycin, nitrofurantoin) and chronic NSAID, including a possible G6PD-type oxidant injury. Also consider DIC (consumptive) and hereditary membrane defects (spherocytosis). The new drugs and the CLL are key confounders. A pregnancy/bleeding source is not the picture here (post-menopausal, no melena).

criticalActions.item: Warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia (likely secondary to CLL)

coreActions.item: Drug-induced / oxidant hemolysis (azithromycin, nitrofurantoin, NSAID, G6PD-type)
Names at least three distinct mechanisms of hemolysis (antibody-mediated, microangiopathic/mechanical, oxidant/drug, consumptive, membrane/enzyme)
States the imperative to look for schistocytes, thrombocytopenia, and renal failure to exclude TTP/HUS before treating

penalties.item: Anchors on iron-deficiency/occult-bleeding anemia and misses the hemolytic picture

acceptableAlternatives: Drug-induced / oxidant hemolysis (azithromycin, nitrofurantoin, NSAID, G6PD-type)

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order to characterize the anemia and triage the can't-miss diagnoses?
steps.rubric: CBC + reticulocyte count to confirm hemolysis with appropriate marrow response (CANT-MISS,3). Peripheral smear — spherocytes vs schistocytes vs bite cells (CANT-MISS,3). Direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test (CANT-MISS,3). Hemolysis labs — LDH, haptoglobin, indirect bilirubin (2). BMP for renal function and electrolytes (catches the thiazide hyponatremia and excludes HUS) (1). Coagulation panel + fibrinogen + D-dimer to exclude DIC (1). EKG given the QT-prolonging drugs (1). CXR (1). PENALTY: starting empiric steroids before excluding TTP/HUS, which needs plasma exchange not steroids (-2); transfusing reflexively without a type-and-screen/DAT-aware crossmatch (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: CBC; Reticulocyte count; Peripheral smear (look specifically for schistocytes vs spherocytes vs bite cells); Direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test; Hemolysis labs (LDH, haptoglobin, indirect bilirubin); BMP (renal function + electrolytes — will catch the thiazide hyponatremia and exclude HUS); Coagulation panel with fibrinogen and D-dimer (exclude DIC); EKG (she is on QT-prolonging azithromycin); CXR. Send a type-and-screen early and warn the blood bank the DAT may be positive, but do NOT give empiric steroids until a microangiopathy is excluded on the smear and platelet/renal panel.

criticalActions.item: Peripheral smear — spherocytes vs schistocytes vs bite cells

coreActions.item: BMP for renal function and electrolytes
Coagulation panel + fibrinogen + D-dimer to exclude DIC

penalties.item: Starting empiric steroids before excluding TTP/HUS (needs plasma exchange, not steroids)
Transfusing reflexively without a type-and-screen / DAT-aware crossmatch

requiredTests: Peripheral smear — spherocytes vs schistocytes vs bite cells
contraindicatedTests: Starting empiric steroids before excluding TTP/HUS (needs plasma exchange, not steroids)
orderSensitiveActions: Starting empiric steroids before excluding TTP/HUS (needs plasma exchange, not steroids)

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's OWN 12-lead EKG, obtained on arrival. Describe your findings and say how they change your medication and electrolyte plan.
steps.rubric: Findings = SINUS TACHYCARDIA (~112 bpm) with a markedly PROLONGED QTc (~505 ms by Bazett; measured QT ~370 ms at this rate); no acute ST-elevation. CREDIT: identifies the prolonged QT/QTc (6); recognizes the tachycardia is a DEMAND response to severe anemia, not a primary arrhythmia (3); attributes the long QT to the QT-prolonging azithromycin plus a low-normal magnesium and states the plan is to STOP the offending drug(s) and repleat Mg/K to prevent torsades (4); systematic read (2). PENALTY: missing the QT prolongation, or adding another QT-prolonging drug (e.g. ondansetron, more macrolide) without correcting electrolytes (-3).
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia (~112 bpm) with a markedly prolonged QTc (~505 ms), no acute ST-elevation.
commonMistakes: reading only the tachycardia and missing the long QT
adding another QT-prolonging drug without repleting magnesium/potassium
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia at ~112 bpm with a markedly prolonged QTc (~505 ms by Bazett; QT ~370 ms at this rate) and broad-based T waves, no acute ST-elevation. The fast rate is a DEMAND response to her severe anemia (Hgb 6.9), not a primary arrhythmia. The long QT is drug-induced — from the recently started azithromycin (a macrolide) compounded by a low-normal magnesium. Management implication: STOP the azithromycin and other QT-prolonging agents, replete magnesium and potassium toward high-normal, avoid adding further QT-prolonging drugs (e.g. ondansetron, haloperidol), and put her on a monitor to watch for torsades while the anemia is treated.

criticalActions: Sinus tachycardia (~112 bpm) with a markedly prolonged QTc (~505 ms), no acute ST-elevation.
coreActions: recognizes tachycardia is demand from severe anemia, not a primary arrhythmia
attributes the long QT to azithromycin + low magnesium and plans to stop the drug and repleat Mg/K

penalties.label: misses the QT prolongation or adds another QT-prolonging drug without correcting electrolytes

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = mild mediastinal/hilar lymphadenopathy (consistent with her CLL) with clear lungs and no edema/effusion. CREDIT: identifies the mediastinal/hilar lymphadenopathy and links it to a lymphoproliferative disorder (6); notes the lungs are clear with no consolidation, effusion, or pulmonary edema as a pertinent negative before transfusing (4). PENALTY: calling the film completely normal / missing the adenopathy, or inventing pneumonia/edema (-2).
keyFindings: clear lungs, no edema or effusion
criticalFindings: Mild mediastinal/hilar lymphadenopathy with clear lungs.
commonMistakes: missing the lymphadenopathy and calling the film normal
inventing an infiltrate or pulmonary edema on a clear film
steps.idealAnswer: Mild mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathy, consistent with her known CLL; lungs are clear with no consolidation, pleural effusion, or pulmonary edema, and a normal cardiac silhouette. The clear lungs are a useful pertinent negative before transfusing this severely anemic patient.

criticalActions: Mild mediastinal/hilar lymphadenopathy with clear lungs.
coreActions: clear lungs, no edema or effusion

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the adenopathy or invents pathology

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Results: reticulocytosis with spherocytes and NO schistocytes, normal platelets, normal renal function, normal coags/fibrinogen, low haptoglobin with high LDH/indirect bilirubin, a strongly POSITIVE IgG Coombs, and an absolute lymphocytosis. What is your final diagnosis, what is the discriminating test, and what did you rule out?
steps.rubric: Warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia, SECONDARY to her CLL (6). Names the positive IgG direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test as THE discriminating test, with hemolysis markers and spherocytes; explicitly rules out TTP/HUS (no schistocytes, normal platelets, normal renal function), DIC (normal PT/aPTT/fibrinogen/D-dimer), and frames the residual ambiguity — primary vs CLL-driven vs a possible drug contribution from the new antibiotics (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Final diagnosis: WARM autoimmune hemolytic anemia (IgG-mediated), most likely SECONDARY to her CLL. The discriminating test is the strongly positive IgG direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test, together with the hemolysis markers (reticulocytosis, undetectable haptoglobin, high LDH and indirect bilirubin) and spherocytes on smear. Ruled out: TTP/HUS (no schistocytes, normal platelets, normal creatinine), DIC (normal PT, aPTT, fibrinogen, and D-dimer), and a purely oxidant/drug hemolysis (no bite cells, and the DAT is positive). Residual ambiguity that should be acknowledged: whether this is primary (idiopathic) warm AIHA, CLL-driven secondary AIHA, or has a drug-induced antibody component from the new azithromycin/nitrofurantoin — which is why the new drugs should be stopped and the CLL re-staged. The mild hyponatremia is a separate, thiazide-related problem, not part of the hemolysis.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including stabilization, the QT issue, and disposition/safe-discharge planning?
steps.rubric: Per MGH hemolytic anemia: corticosteroids first-line for warm AIHA, e.g. prednisone 1 mg/kg (CANT-MISS,6); STOP the offending/contributing drugs — azithromycin and nitrofurantoin (and review the thiazide/NSAID) and avoid further QT-prolonging agents, repleat magnesium/potassium with telemetry for the prolonged QTc (CANT-MISS,4); folate supplementation for the brisk hemolysis (2); transfuse least-incompatible/best-match units for symptomatic severe anemia, warn the blood bank of the positive DAT, do NOT withhold a life-saving transfusion for crossmatch incompatibility (3); treat the underlying CLL / hematology consult for secondary AIHA and possible rituximab (3); correct the thiazide-related hyponatremia by stopping the thiazide (1); arrange SAFE DISPOSITION — admit for severe symptomatic anemia + prolonged QT on telemetry, medication reconciliation, and on discharge ensure steroid taper plan, repeat CBC/reticulocyte and hemolysis labs, repeat EKG to confirm QTc normalized, and close hematology follow-up (4). PENALTY: discharging a Hgb 6.9 patient with a prolonged QTc home (-4); withholding life-saving transfusion for crossmatch incompatibility (-3); continuing the QT-prolonging antibiotic (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Stabilize and admit (severe symptomatic anemia plus a prolonged QTc needs telemetry). Start corticosteroids first-line for warm AIHA (prednisone ~1 mg/kg) once TTP/HUS is excluded. STOP the contributing drugs: azithromycin and nitrofurantoin (and hold the thiazide and the daily NSAID); avoid any further QT-prolonging agents and replete magnesium and potassium toward high-normal under telemetry to prevent torsades; repeat the EKG to confirm the QTc is correcting. Add folate for the brisk hemolysis. Transfuse for symptomatic severe anemia using the best-matched/least-incompatible units and warn the blood bank the DAT is positive — do NOT withhold a life-saving transfusion over crossmatch incompatibility. Engage hematology: treat the underlying CLL as the driver of this secondary AIHA and consider rituximab if refractory or relapsing; the response to treating the CLL helps resolve the residual primary-vs-secondary ambiguity. Stop the thiazide to correct the mild hyponatremia. Safe disposition / discharge planning: medication reconciliation to remove the offending and QT-prolonging drugs, a documented steroid taper plan, repeat CBC/reticulocyte and hemolysis labs to confirm the count is recovering, a repeat EKG to confirm QTc normalization before discharge, patient counseling to avoid the implicated antibiotics in future, and close hematology follow-up.

criticalActions.item: Stop offending/QT-prolonging drugs (azithromycin, nitrofurantoin), repleat Mg/K with telemetry for the prolonged QTc

coreActions.item: Transfuse least-incompatible/best-match units for symptomatic severe anemia (warn blood bank of positive DAT)
Treat underlying CLL / hematology consult for secondary AIHA (consider rituximab)
Stop the thiazide to correct the hyponatremia
Arrange safe disposition: admit with telemetry, medication reconciliation, steroid taper plan, repeat CBC/retic + EKG to confirm QTc, close hematology follow-up

penalties.item: Discharging a Hgb 6.9 patient with a prolonged QTc home
Withholding life-saving transfusion for crossmatch incompatibility

requiredActions: Stop offending/QT-prolonging drugs (azithromycin, nitrofurantoin), repleat Mg/K with telemetry for the prolonged QTc
optionalActions: Transfuse least-incompatible/best-match units for symptomatic severe anemia (warn blood bank of positive DAT)
Treat underlying CLL / hematology consult for secondary AIHA (consider rituximab)
Stop the thiazide to correct the hyponatremia
Arrange safe disposition: admit with telemetry, medication reconciliation, steroid taper plan, repeat CBC/retic + EKG to confirm QTc, close hematology follow-up
unsafeActions: Discharging a Hgb 6.9 patient with a prolonged QTc home
Withholding life-saving transfusion for crossmatch incompatibility

physicalExamMappings.finding: Lymph nodes: a few small, soft, non-tender enlarged cervical and axillary nodes (chronic, from her CLL).
Abdomen: moderate splenomegaly if examined; no marked tenderness; liver not enlarged.
Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, soft systolic flow murmur. No petechiae/purpura; no focal neuro deficit.
Cardiac: no petechiae, NO purpura, no new bruising (argues against thrombocytopenia/TTP).

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 62F with known CLL (watchful-waiting), hypertension on a thiazide, and recently started antibiotics (azithromycin, nitrofurantoin) plus a chronic NSAID presents with one week of rapidly worsening fatigue, jaundice, and dark urine. The diagnosis is warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia, secondary to her CLL, confirmed by a positive IgG Coombs with a hemolytic pattern and spherocytes. It is reached by eliciting the focused history (recent illness, new drugs, CLL, family history), performing the targeted exam (lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, pallor, jaundice, no purpura), interpreting an integrated EKG that shows a drug-induced prolonged QTc on a demand tachycardia, and excluding the must-not-miss microangiopathy (TTP/HUS) and DIC. Concurrent thiazide-related hyponatremia and a drug-induced long QT are managed as separate problems.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Lymph nodes: a few small, soft, non-tender enlarged cervical and axillary nodes (chronic, from her CLL).
Abdomen: moderate splenomegaly if examined; no marked tenderness; liver not enlarged.
Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, soft systolic flow murmur. No petechiae/purpura; no focal neuro deficit.
Cardiac: no petechiae, NO purpura, no new bruising (argues against thrombocytopenia/TTP).
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals (severe anemia, tachycardia, jaundice).
Elicit the focused history, including the recent illness, new antibiotics, the thiazide/NSAID, the CLL, and family history only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, pallor, jaundice, and the absence of purpura.
Build a differential led by warm AIHA (likely CLL-driven) while explicitly excluding the must-not-miss microangiopathy (TTP/HUS) and DIC, and naming at least three mechanisms of hemolysis.
Order a focused workup (CBC/retic, smear, DAT, hemolysis labs, BMP, coags) and interpret the integrated EKG showing a drug-induced prolonged QTc and the CXR showing lymphadenopathy.

Commit to secondary warm AIHA and manage with steroids, drug withdrawal + QTc/electrolyte correction, folate, careful transfusion, CLL-directed therapy, and a safe telemetry admission with discharge planning.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on iron-deficiency/occult-bleeding anemia and missing the hemolytic picture
Missing the drug-induced prolonged QTc or adding another QT-prolonging drug
Discharging a severely anemic patient with a long QT
Withholding life-saving transfusion for crossmatch incompatibility
managementPearls: Stop the offending/QT-prolonging drugs and repleat Mg/K under telemetry
Treat the underlying CLL as the driver of secondary AIHA
criticalWorkupItems: Peripheral smear — spherocytes vs schistocytes vs bite cells
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia (~112 bpm) with a markedly prolonged QTc (~505 ms), no acute ST-elevation.
Mild mediastinal/hilar lymphadenopathy with clear lungs.
criticalManagementItems: Stop offending/QT-prolonging drugs (azithromycin, nitrofurantoin), repleat Mg/K with telemetry for the prolonged QTc
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia (~112 bpm) with a markedly prolonged QTc (~505 ms), no acute ST-elevation.
Mild mediastinal/hilar lymphadenopathy with clear lungs.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia (~112 bpm) with a markedly prolonged QTc (~505 ms), no acute ST-elevation.
Mild mediastinal/hilar lymphadenopathy with clear lungs.
imageInterpretationPearls: A demand tachycardia in a severely anemic patient is sinus, not a primary arrhythmia — but still read the intervals: a long QT on a QT-prolonging drug is the dangerous finding.
Tie the EKG back to the medication list and electrolytes — stop the offender and repleat Mg/K before torsades.
Read every study systematically and state pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
